name: Emery Phenomenon
identifyBy: Junctional rhythm with inverted P waves (retrograde atrial depolarisation)
Short PR interval or P wave buried in/after QRS
Apparent ST elevation in inferior leads (II, III, aVF) that is actually the Ta wave (atrial repolarisation)
Ta wave (atrial T wave) appears in the ST segment - normally hidden but visible when P wave is inverted
ST elevation resolves when rhythm reverts to sinus (diagnostic clue)

measurements.stSegment: Apparent elevation in inferior leads - actually Ta wave artefact

management: Emery phenomenon itself requires no treatment - it is a benign mimicker
The underlying junctional rhythm may need treatment if symptomatic
Critical: Do not treat as STEMI - serial ECGs and clinical correlation are essential
Return to sinus rhythm will abolish the 'ST elevation' (diagnostic)
Causes of junctional rhythm: vagal tone, inferior MI, post-cardiac surgery, sick sinus syndrome, drug toxicity
If uncertain: immediate cardiology review and echocardiogram
pearls: Emery phenomenon: the Ta wave (atrial repolarisation wave) is normally opposite in polarity to the P wave
In junctional rhythm, inverted P → Ta wave is positive in inferior leads → mimics ST elevation
Classic scenario: elderly patient in junctional rhythm, 'inferior STEMI' found in ED, but serial ECGs show resolution when rate increases to sinus
Differentiates from STEMI: no wall motion abnormality, normal troponin, ST resolves with sinus conversion
Named after Emery who described the Ta wave causing artefactual ST changes - rare but important STEMI mimic
differentials: Inferior STEMI (convex ST, reciprocal aVL depression, wall motion abnormality, troponin rise)
High nodal junctional with ischaemia (actual ST changes co-exist)
Hyperacute T waves inferior (early STEMI - needs serial ECG)
